moduleTitle: Cardiac Biophysics & Muscle Mechanics Biophysical & Cellular Mechanics

concepts.conceptName: Myocyte vs. Pacemaker Action Potential Biophysics

ventricularMyocytePhases.Phase_0_Depolarization: Inward $Na^+$ current ($I_{Na}$) via voltage-gated $Nav1.5$ channels. Conduction velocity: ~0.3-1.0 m/s.
ventricularMyocytePhases.Phase_2_Plateau: Inward $Ca^{2+}$ current ($I_{Ca,L}$) via L-type calcium channels (Cav1.2/DHPR) balancing outward delayed rectifier $K^+$ currents ($I_{Kr}$, $I_{Ks}$). Duration: 200-300 ms.
ventricularMyocytePhases.Phase_3_Rapid_Repolarization: Outward delayed rectifier $K^+$ currents ($I_{Kr}$, $I_{Ks}$) dominate as $I_{Ca,L}$ channels close.
ventricularMyocytePhases.Phase_4_Resting_Potential: Stable resting membrane potential (typically -85 to -90 mV) maintained by the inward rectifier $K^+$ current ($I_{K1}$).

pacemakerPhases_SA_AV_Node.Phase_4_Diastolic_Depolarization: Unstable resting potential starting at -60 mV. Spontaneous depolarization driven by: 1. Funny current ($I_f$; inward mixed $Na^+$/$K^+$ current via HCN channels); 2. $T$-type $Ca^{2+}$ current ($I_{Ca,T}$); 3. $L$-type $Ca^{2+}$ current ($I_{Ca,L}$) near threshold.
pacemakerPhases_SA_AV_Node.Phase_0_Depolarization: Upstroke driven entirely by inward $Ca^{2+}$ current ($I_{Ca,L}$) via Cav1.2 channels. Conduction velocity: ~0.01-0.05 m/s (slow AV node delay).

refractoryPeriods.effectiveRefractoryPeriod_ERP: Extends from Phase 0 to midway through Phase 3 (~200 ms). It is physically impossible to propagate a second action potential, preventing tetanic contraction.
refractoryPeriods.relativeRefractoryPeriod_RRP: Spans the remainder of Phase 3, where a localized, non-propagated electrical response can be generated by a strong suprathreshold stimulus.

firstPrinciplesMechanics.plateauPhasePhysiology: The exceptionally long plateau phase (Phase 2, ~200-300 ms) of the ventricular action potential is a unique evolutionary adaptation that prevents tetany (sustained contraction) of the myocardium, ensuring the heart can relax and fill during diastole. Biophysically, as the membrane depolarizes during Phase 0, the voltage-gated L-type calcium channels (Cav1.2/DHPR) open slowly. This inward, depolarizing $Ca^{2+}$ current ($I_{Ca,L}$) acts as a counterbalance to the concurrent activation of outward, repolarizing delayed rectifier potassium currents ($I_{Kr}$ and $I_{Ks}$). This precise electrostatic equilibrium maintains the membrane in a depolarized, plateau state. Only when the $I_{Ca,L}$ channels spontaneously inactivate does repolarization proceed, matching the duration of the electrical action potential to the duration of mechanical myofibrillar contraction.
firstPrinciplesMechanics.pacemakerSpontaneousDepolarization: Unlike working myocytes, pacemaker cells in the SA and AV nodes lack a stable resting membrane potential due to the complete absence of $I_{K1}$ inward rectifier channels. Consequently, Phase 4 is a dynamic, continuous depolarization (pacemaker potential) driven by three biophysical systems: 1. **The Funny Current ($I_f$):** Conducted by Hyperpolarization-Activated Cyclic Nucleotide-gated (HCN) channels. These unique channels are activated when the membrane repolarizes below $-50\text{ mV}$ during Phase 3, driving a slow, inward $Na^+$ and $K^+$ current; 2. **The Calcium Clock:** As the funny current depolarizes the membrane past $-50\text{ mV}$, low-voltage activated $T\text{-type}$ calcium channels open, generating a transient inward $Ca^{2+}$ current ($I_{Ca,T}$); 3. **The Membrane Clock:** Sub-threshold sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) $Ca^{2+}$ release activates the $Na^+/Ca^{2+}$ exchanger (NCX), driving a net depolarizing inward current ($I_{NCX}$) that brings the membrane to $-40\text{ mV}$, activating $L\text{-type}$ $Ca^{2+}$ channels to initiate Phase 0.
firstPrinciplesMechanics.conductionVelocityMechanics: Conduction velocity through cardiac tissue is governed by passive cable properties and the rate of upstroke depolarization ($dV/dt_{max}$). 1. **Working Myocytes:** High density of $Nav1.5$ channels generates an explosive $I_{Na}$ during Phase 0, producing a massive $dV/dt_{max}$ ($>200\text{ V/s}$) and rapid propagation; 2. **Nodal Cells:** Lack $Nav1.5$ channels; Phase 0 is driven by the slow activation kinetics of $I_{Ca,L}$. This results in a very flat upstroke ($dV/dt_{max}$ of only $10\text{ V/s}$), producing the slow conduction velocity necessary to introduce the critical $0.1\text{ s}$ delay at the AV node, allowing the atria to fully empty into the ventricles.

clinicalAndAnaestheticCorrelations.anatomicalBasis: Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (such as **Verapamil** and **Diltiazem**) selectively bind to the intracellular pore-forming $\alpha_1$ subunit of the Cav1.2 L-type calcium channel. By directly reducing $I_{Ca,L}$, they produce three distinct biophysical effects: 1. **Negative Inotropy:** Reduces the trigger $Ca^{2+}$ required for sarcoplasmic reticulum calcium-induced calcium release, depressing contractility; 2. **Negative Chronotropy:** Flattens the slope of Phase 4 pacemaker depolarization in the SA node; 3. **Negative Dromotropy:** Significantly flattens the Phase 0 upstroke slope ($dV/dt_{max}$) in the AV node, prolonging the PR interval and potentially triggering high-grade AV block.

clinicalAndAnaestheticCorrelations.scenario: Hyperkalaemic Peaked T Waves and Arrhythmias
clinicalAndAnaestheticCorrelations.anatomicalBasis: According to the GHK equation, rising extracellular potassium ($[K^+]_o$) depolarizes the resting membrane potential. This depolarization inactivates the $h$-gates of the fast $Nav1.5$ channels, drastically reducing Phase 0 $dV/dt_{max}$ and slowing conduction. Concurrently, elevated $[K^+]_o$ allosterically increases the conductance of the rapid delayed rectifier potassium channels ($I_{Kr}$). This accelerates Phase 3 repolarization, shortening the overall action potential duration and narrowing the repolarization window, which manifests on the ECG as the classic symmetrical **peaked T waves** of hyperkalaemia, progressing to QRS widening and a sinusoidal rhythm.

roteData.sarcomereStructure: The basic functional unit of myofibrillar contraction. Bounded by two Z-discs. Contains: 1. Thick filaments (Myosin; localized to the A-band); 2. Thin filaments (Actin, Troponin, Tropomyosin; localized to the I-band and interdigitating into the A-band); 3. Titin (anchors myosin to the Z-disc, providing elastic passive tension).
roteData.troponinComplex: 1. **Troponin C (TnC):** The calcium-binding subunit; contains one active low-affinity regulatory $Ca^{2+}$ binding site in cardiac muscle (compared to two in skeletal muscle); 2. **Troponin I (TnI):** The inhibitory subunit; physically blocks the actin-myosin binding site; 3. **Troponin T (TnT):** Binds the troponin complex to tropomyosin.
roteData.calciumConcentrationDynamics: Diastolic (resting) free intracellular $[Ca^{2+}]_i \approx 100\text{ nM}$. Systolic (peak) free intracellular $[Ca^{2+}]_i \approx 10\text{ }\mu\text{M}$ (a 100-fold increase).

firstPrinciplesMechanics.calciumInducedCalciumRelease_CICR: Excitation-contraction coupling represents the conversion of an electrical action potential into mechanical force. 1. **Trigger Calcium Influx:** The action potential propagates down the sarcolemma and deep into the transverse tubules (T-tubules). During the Phase 2 plateau, the depolarization opens L-type calcium channels (Cav1.2/DHPR), driving an inward $Ca^{2+}$ current ($I_{Ca,L}$); 2. **RyR2 Activation:** This trigger $Ca^{2+}$ enters the microscopic junctional cleft (the dyadic space) separating the T-tubule membrane from the junctional sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR). The high local concentration ($10\text{ to }100\text{ }\mu\text{M}$) binds to and opens **Ryanodine Receptors (RyR2)** on the SR terminal cisternae; 3. **Amplification:** The RyR2 channels release a massive pool of stored $Ca^{2+}$ from the SR lumen into the cytoplasm, amplifying the initial trigger signal by up to 10-fold. This is the biophysical process of **Calcium-Induced Calcium Release (CICR)**.
firstPrinciplesMechanics.molecularThinFilamentActivation: The released free $Ca^{2+}$ diffuses into the myofibrillar space and binds to the single regulatory site on **Troponin C (TnC)**. This binding induces a conformational shift that increases the hydrophobic interaction between TnC and **Troponin I (TnI)**, pulling TnI away from the actin filament. This movement is transmitted via **Troponin T (TnT)** to **Tropomyosin**, dragging the long tropomyosin polymer approximately $1.5\text{ nm}$ deeper into the actin helix groove. This physically uncovers and exposes the myosin-binding sites on the actin monomers, allowing cross-bridge formation.
firstPrinciplesMechanics.crossBridgeThermodynamics: The force-generating cross-bridge cycle operates through four distinct conformational states: 1. **Attachment:** The myosin head (pre-loaded with ADP and inorganic phosphate, $P_i$) binds to the exposed actin site, forming a cross-bridge; 2. **Power Stroke:** The binding of actin triggers the immediate release of $P_i$ from the myosin head, inducing a structural rotation of the converter region and neck (the power stroke). This pulls the actin filament approximately $10\text{ nm}$ toward the center of the sarcomere (generating force and shortening). ADP is subsequently released, leaving the cross-bridge in a stable, low-energy 'rigor' conformation; 3. **Detachment:** A new molecule of ATP binds to the nucleotide pocket on the myosin head. This allosterically slashes myosin's affinity for actin, causing immediate cross-bridge dissociation; 4. **Recocking:** The intrinsic ATPase activity of the myosin head hydrolyzes the bound ATP into ADP and $P_i$. The energy released from this hydrolysis is stored as potential energy, physically recocking the myosin head back into its high-energy conformation, ready to repeat the cycle.

clinicalAndAnaestheticCorrelations.anatomicalBasis: In severe heart failure, **Levosimendan** acts as a calcium sensitizer. It binds directly to the regulatory domain of **Troponin C** in a calcium-dependent manner, stabilizing the conformation of the $Ca^{2+}\text{-TnC}$ complex once calcium is bound. This prevents the premature dissociation of calcium from TnC during systole, prolonging the open state of the actin-myosin binding site. Consequently, it increases inotropy (force generation) without raising intracellular calcium concentrations ($[Ca^{2+}]_i$), avoiding the increased myocardial oxygen consumption ($MVO_2$) and arrhythmogenic risks associated with calcium-overloading inotropes (such as adrenaline or milrinone).

Volatile anaesthetics (e.g., **Isoflurane**, **Sevoflurane**) dose-dependently depress myocardial contractility. The primary biophysical mechanism is a direct reduction in the L-type calcium channel current ($I_{Ca,L}$), which decreases the trigger calcium entering the dyadic cleft. This reduces the probability of RyR2 opening and blunts the amplitude of the sarcoplasmic reticulum calcium transient during CICR, resulting in fewer active cross-bridges and decreased stroke volume.

calciumClearancePathways.SERCA2a: Responsible for clearing ~70\% of cytoplasmic calcium in human ventricles; actively pumps $Ca^{2+}$ back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum lumen.
calciumClearancePathways.NCX_exchanger: Responsible for clearing ~28\% of calcium; electrogenically pumps $Ca^{2+}$ out of the sarcolemma into the extracellular space.
calciumClearancePathways.PMCA_and_Mitochondria: Responsible for clearing the remaining ~2\%; slow, low-capacity sarcolemmal calcium ATPase and mitochondrial calcium uniporter.

roteData.serca2a_stoichiometry: Pumps $2\text{ Ca}^{2+}$ ions from the cytoplasm into the SR lumen in exchange for $2\text{-}3\text{ H}^+$ ions, consuming $1\text{ ATP}$ molecule.
roteData.ncx_stoichiometry: Pumps $1\text{ Ca}^{2+}$ ion OUT of the cell in exchange for $3\text{ Na}^+$ ions entering the cell down their electrochemical gradient.

firstPrinciplesMechanics.lusitropyAndSerca2aKineticControl: Lusitropy represents the active, energy-consuming process of myocardial relaxation. For diastole to commence, intracellular $[Ca^{2+}]_i$ must be rapidly cleared below $100\text{ nM}$ to allow calcium to dissociate from Troponin C, terminating cross-bridge cycling. The dominant clearance engine is the **Sarcoplasmic/Endoplasmic Reticulum Calcium ATPase 2a (SERCA2a)**. The baseline pumping velocity of SERCA2a is tonically suppressed and braked by **Phospholamban (PLN)**, an unphosphorylated transmembrane SR protein that binds directly to SERCA2a, reducing its affinity for calcium.
firstPrinciplesMechanics.phospholambanRegulationLoop: During sympathetic stimulation, noradrenaline binds to myocardial $\beta_1$ adrenergic receptors, activating $G_s$, which stimulates adenylyl cyclase to increase cyclic AMP (cAMP). This activates **Protein Kinase A (PKA)**. PKA phosphorylates Phospholamban at **Serine-16** (while high intracellular calcium concurrently activates CaMKII, which phosphorylates PLN at **Threonine-17**). Upon phosphorylation, PLN undergoes a conformational change and dissociates from SERCA2a, removing the brake. This increases SERCA2a's affinity for calcium, dramatically accelerating the rate of $Ca^{2+}$ reuptake into the SR lumen. This accelerates relaxation (**positive lusitropy**), which is physiologically essential during tachycardia to shorten diastole proportionally and preserve ventricular filling time.
firstPrinciplesMechanics.ncxElectrogenicFlux: The **Sodium-Calcium Exchanger (NCX)** is a secondary active transporter. It does not consume ATP directly; instead, it utilizes the steep sodium gradient ($[Na^+]_o \approx 140\text{ mM}$ vs. $[Na^+]_i \approx 14\text{ mM}$) maintained by the $Na^+/K^+$ ATPase pump. Because it exchanges $3\text{ Na}^+$ ions ($3\text{ positive charges}$) inward for only $1\text{ Ca}^{2+}$ ion ($2\text{ positive charges}$) outward, it is **electrogenic**. This inward movement of $1\text{ net positive charge}$ generates an inward depolarizing current ($I_{NCX}$) during late systole and early diastole, which can trigger delayed afterdepolarizations (DADs) in states of intracellular calcium overload.

clinicalAndAnaestheticCorrelations.anatomicalBasis: Myocardial relaxation (lusitropy) is an active, ATP-consuming process. In patients with diastolic heart failure, **Milrinone** acts as a selective Phosphodiesterase-III (PDE-III) inhibitor, preventing the intracellular breakdown of cAMP. This sustains elevated PKA activity, driving continuous phosphorylation of **Phospholamban**. By permanently removing the PLN brake on **SERCA2a**, milrinone accelerates calcium reuptake into the SR. This produces a profound positive lusitropic effect (accelerating diastolic relaxation, reducing left ventricular end-diastolic pressure, and improving filling) while simultaneously increasing inotropy and causing systemic vasodilation (inodilator).

Digoxin selectively and reversibly binds to the extracellular alpha-subunit of the sarcolemmal **$Na^+/K^+$ ATPase pump**, inhibiting its active transport. This leads to a localized accumulation of intracellular sodium, reducing the steepness of the $Na^+$ concentration gradient. Biophysically, this collapses the thermodynamic driving force for the **NCX exchanger**, preventing it from pumping $Ca^{2+}$ out of the cell. Intracellular $[Ca^{2+}]_i$ accumulates and is pumped by SERCA2a into the SR, increasing the calcium load available for subsequent CICR, enhancing inotropy. However, if $[Ca^{2+}]_i$ accumulates excessively, spontaneous SR calcium release during Phase 4 activates the electrogenic NCX, driving a large inward $I_{NCX}$ current that depolarizes the membrane (Delayed Afterdepolarizations, DADs), manifesting as ventricular ectopy or bigeminy.

roteData.optimalSarcomereLength: 2.2 micrometers (maximal overlap of thick and thin filaments; produces peak active tension).
roteData.sarcomereLimits: Working physiological range is ~1.8 to 2.2 micrometers. Tension drops precipitously outside these boundaries.
roteData.titinStiffness: Titin acts as a molecular spring, generating passive tension when sarcomeres are stretched.

firstPrinciplesMechanics.filamentOverlapGeometry: The relationship between preload and contractile force (the Frank-Starling Law) is fundamentally rooted in sarcomere geometry. 1. **At short lengths ($<1.8\ \mu\text{m}$):** Thin filaments overlap and collide in the center of the sarcomere, interfering with cross-bridge formation. The lateral distance between thick and thin filaments is also increased, reducing binding probability; 2. **At optimal length ($2.2\ \mu\text{m}$):** Interdigitating thick and thin filaments achieve maximum steric overlap, maximizing the number of potential cross-bridge linkages; 3. **At long lengths ($>2.4\ \mu\text{m}$):** Filaments pull apart, reducing overlap and decreasing active force generation.
firstPrinciplesMechanics.lengthDependentCalciumSensitivity: The primary biophysical driver of the Frank-Starling mechanism is **length-dependent calcium sensitivity** of Troponin C (TnC). Stretching the cardiomyocyte (increasing preload) induces a structural distortion of the TnC molecule that increases its affinity for $Ca^{2+}$, lowering the concentration of calcium required to trigger cross-bridge cycling. Concurrently, stretching pulls the sarcolemma taut, narrowing the cell diameter and reducing the lateral distance between thick and thin filaments. This increases the probability of myosin heads finding and binding to actin sites.
firstPrinciplesMechanics.titinPassiveSpring: Titin molecules run from the Z-disc to the M-line, acting as elastic springs. When preload stretches the sarcomere, titin generates passive tension. This passive tension acts as a restorative force and also structurally aligns the myosin heads to optimize their attachment angle to actin during the subsequent contraction.

clinicalAndAnaestheticCorrelations.scenario: Congestive Heart Failure and Descending Limb of Starling Curve
clinicalAndAnaestheticCorrelations.anatomicalBasis: In chronic dilated cardiomyopathy, volume overload stretches the ventricular myocytes. Sarcomere lengths are pushed beyond the optimal $2.2\ \mu\text{m}$ limit ($>2.4\ \mu\text{m}$). Biophysically, this decreases the overlap between thick and thin filaments and increases the lateral spacing between them. The cardiac muscle is forced onto the 'descending limb' of the Starling curve, where further increases in end-diastolic volume (preload) fail to increase stroke volume and instead lead to decreased contractility, elevated LVEDP, and pulmonary congestion.

roteData.primaryCardiacConnexin: Connexin-43 (Cx43; localized primarily in ventricular working myocytes).
roteData.intercalatedDiscComponents: 1. Fascia Adherens (anchors actin filaments); 2. Macula Adherens / Desmosomes (anchors intermediate filaments); 3. Gap Junctions (electrical channels).

firstPrinciplesMechanics.electricalCoupling: cardiac muscle operates as a functional electrical syncytium. Cardiomyocytes are physically and electrically joined at their longitudinal ends by **intercalated discs**. The discs run transversely (housing mechanical anchors) and longitudinally (housing electrical channels). The **gap junctions** are formed by two aligned connexons (one from each cell) spanning the intercellular gap, creating a continuous water-filled channel with an inner diameter of $\approx 1.5-2\text{ nm}$.
firstPrinciplesMechanics.lowResistancePathways: Because gap junctions allow free diffusion of inorganic ions (like $Na^+$, $K^+$, $Ca^{2+}$), they present an exceptionally low electrical resistance ($r_i$). When an action potential depolarizes a cardiomyocyte, the local positive charge (mostly $Na^+$) flows passively and instantly through the gap junctions into the adjacent cell, bringing its membrane to threshold. This eliminates the need for chemical synaptic transmission at each junction, allowing the action potential to propagate rapidly as a continuous wave.
firstPrinciplesMechanics.mechanicalAnchoring: To withstand the massive shear forces of contraction without tearing, intercalated discs contain: 1. **Fascia Adherens:** Connects the actin cytoskeleton of adjacent cells, serving as the mechanical insertion site for the terminal sarcomere; 2. **Desmosomes:** Bind the intermediate desmin filaments together, maintaining structural integrity.

clinicalAndAnaestheticCorrelations.scenario: Ischemic Arrhythmogenesis and Gap Junction Uncoupling
clinicalAndAnaestheticCorrelations.anatomicalBasis: During acute myocardial ischemia, the lack of ATP halts active transport, causing intracellular accumulation of $Ca^{2+}$ and $H^+$ (acidosis). Elevated intracellular calcium ($[Ca^{2+}]_i$) and low pH directly trigger a conformational closure (gate shut) of the **Connexin-43** channels. This electrically uncouples the ischemic cells from the surrounding healthy myocardium. While this isolates the damaged area to prevent metabolic leak, it dramatically increases electrical resistance ($r_i$). This slows conduction velocity and creates localized pathways for re-entry circuits, directly predisposing the patient to ventricular fibrillation.
